sentences.text: We went to a town by the name of Klotz.
This was ex-German town.
In Polish it spells Klotz.
It was K-O-- Klotz.
K-L-O-T-Z, Klotz.
Actually we stopped in Wrowslav, which was a German city by the name Breslau.
And this was about 50 kilometers.
We went over there, to Klotz.
In Klotz, we went close-- this was the border town within Czechoslovakia already.
We went a little bit by truck, not by bus.
It was a regular truck.
Over there we went down, and it was-- I remember, like now, there was plenty snow.
It must've been in February--
February, March, 1945.
And we walked maybe two, three hours through the woods.
Which I had no difficult of maneuvering around the woods already.
[LAUGHS] And we came into a town in Czechoslovakia, by the name Nove Mesto.
Nove Mesto's like a new city, it would be in English.
But in Czech it was the Nove Mesto.
From there, our leaders had Czech money, krons.
They bought tickets.
And we went in to Prague.
Yeah.
This was the-- we went with the organization, with the kibbutz.
We were, about, I think, 27.
Later join us another group.
We were about 30, 35, 36.
All youth.
This was-- they had help from the America, here, from the Jews, the American Jews.
And actually, all the help and the-- and the food and the clothing and everything was-- came from here.
And the organizers were ex and not-ex Haganah members from the Israeli underground.
We were daytime, normal members from the kibbutz.
Nighttime we were at military training.
So this continued all the time, like this.
So we came-- from Nove Mesto we went to Prague.
And Prague, we were only were one day.
And we went to Bratislava.
I remember even the street name-- [?
Yuzafovas ?] 17 was the place.
There was a Jewish house over there.
They kept us till the night.
In the night, we went through the border to Austria.
Austria, we stayed at-- in Vienna.
And the building, what they call it, Rothschilds House.
The buildings what from now are famous, Rothschilds, belong.
And that, over there, we were maybe about 500, 600 youths already, getting together from all the corners.
The food was very survival.
Not special good.
UNRRA was given there.
This was, already, the United Nation help, that, over there.
And from there we went-- actually, before we went, being outside by the building, Austrian police passed by.
And they said, one to the other, in German, [GERMAN].
This is like, in English, rotten Jews are here.
I understood them.
And not only understood them, it worked up many feeling from before.
And several boys and girls were outside.
We all understood.
And we grabbed them with-- we give them a lesson on the spot.
And then they get help.
Other came in.
And the end of the story, we went out, all our 500 or whatever, we went.
We chased them up to the police station.
It was a six-story building.
And we demolished, inside from the building, everything.
All the telephones, the desks, the file cabinets, through the windows, were flying down in the street.
Next to me was one boxer, older from me.
Whoever he grabbed, he pulled a guy, gave him what in the-- in the eyes.
And the-- the blood from the guy, from the police guy, I was completely red.
[LAUGHS] Needless to say, after a while, the American MP arrested us.
He gave us over to the Austrian police.
And they put us in jail.
So we were sitting six days, Passover, exactly. 1945?
Yeah. '45.
I think that was '45, that time.
Passover, we get matzos to the-- to jail.
And they released us with one condition, never to come back to Austria.
And the Jewish, the underground supplied certificates, that we have certificates to go to Israel.
And we went on a train that we're going to Israel.
As of the left Vienna, we arrived Linz-Bindermichel.
That was Jewish camps over there.
In Austria.
Not camps like, [? in town. ?] But camps from the people, what survived in the camps.
Survival camp, was this one.
DP camps.
With-- came in over there.
And the guy said, on your certificate, somebody else will go to Israel.
You are all healthy to go through the underground way.
[LAUGHS]
So, that's how we wind up in Austria.
We were a short time where we sign, us, a thing that we cannot-- that we leave Austria.
So we cannot stay too long, the one from the jail.
We were 17 boys and 4 girls arrested.
Actual 21, a group.
[LAUGHS]
So they-- we went to the-- through Salzburg, near the Italian border.
We walked the whole night through the mountains.
The mosquito bites, everybody had bites, unbelievable.
We couldn't stand for the mosquitoes.
And until we passed to the Italian side.
In the other side, there were military trucks waiting for us from the Jewish Brigade, serving in English Army [? these ?] [? were. ?] And they took us.
They closed up the military trucks.
And the drove into Milano, and we went from Milano by train to [?
Aviano. ?] And that's how it started, over there, the school.
Yeah, they were.
Well, me plus other ones, we wind up in the school, about 500 kids.
Actually, they were all friends.
All of them.
Unfortunately, the survival from them-- from the school was one-- one battle, where we had eight hours a day, classes, and four hours homework.
And Sunday was one day off.
It was involved with Haganah training or packing weapons, what we got from Czechoslovakia, to go on the torpedo bus, to ship to Israel.
We were always active with this.
But at the Haganah training, was separate from the school.
So even the dropouts from school were still members from the underground training.
And--
Yes?
Mostly we studied this mathematic and Hebrew language.
It was two.
Very little geography or history, must learn the two items.
But the majority of kids, included me, didn't know how to write or read.
Simply start like a-- from one class finish in Poland, and we started in Hebrew language.
I understood a little bit from the time when I went to cheder and learn to daven.
And so it was easier.
Still some of them didn't know nothing.
It was-- everything was in Hebrew.
One thing to live in Israel.
Second to-- there were, from Romania, kids.
From Hungary, from Czechoslovakia.
You cannot go in every separate language.
So let's do our language, one, is Hebrew, and that's it.
I think it was a very wise decision.
In the beginning, it was difficult.
But later on, everybody start talking between them in Hebrew.
From the beginning, it was to learn how to sing in Hebrew, Hebrew songs.
And then was-- but at the exams, after two years, we left less 320.
Of the 500.
But they were all involved in the kibbutz life, in the underground.
They were doing other work when we were doing this.
They were more transporting the weapons, what we needed, to Israel.
They were active all the time.
Even they didn't attend the school, but they were there.
And I knew each one of them.
At-- the end of 1947, when I ask permission to leave for two, three months to get mother out, they gave me permission.
And I could not succeed of getting mother out from Poland.
Actually, they gave me a hard time when I arrived.
What I did, two years overseas?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And for whoever, spying.
Finally I was lucky not to go to jail at that moment.
But when I was seeing I cannot succeed, legally, to take mother.
And through the border, to go with mother was impossible.
And at that time, I knew my time is up.
I have to come back to the unit.
I decided to smuggle the border by myself.
So I went to the same city, Klotz.
Over there, find out that their leaders, what they are-- smugglers, what they're taking over to the other-side border, for money.
And we were six.
Six here.
I combine with six other guys to go through the border.
This was Czech people, were taking over the border, the other side.
The system was like this.
You took a picture.
You ripped it half.
Half you left by friend with the money for the border.
I think it was $150 US money too.
If the guy came back with the picture, with the half picture, you matched it, the person, which you left the money, pay them.
But everyone had his own-- had his own friend.
Nobody lived by the same people.
But this was in danger.
You had to have a good friend to accept it, to do it like this.
The reason was not only the money, that you can lose it.
The fact is that something can happen to you.
Who know with who you're going, at least.
Until he doesn't come back with the picture, you don't know if you actually went through the border.
So I went with this group.
And as I passed before Nove Mesto, the same route what I went two years earlier, I recognized, in the woods behind the trees, that the military men standing with their guns.
I had good eyes from the time when I went through the woods.
And at that moment, I ripped apart the half a picture for small little pieces.
And I had Czech money with me.
I throw it away before they-- outside money, they call it, was special jail.
More like anything else.
They call it-- you took out the-- the visa, they called it.
So I throw away the money and I rip the picture in pieces.
And it didn't take long.
And they screamed right away, hands up.
And I knew, already, there's no way to escape.
We were surrounded.
[LAUGHS]
So they took us, that time, to Nove Mesto, over there.
The Czech kept us three days.
And they put us in a military truck and brought it back to Poland.
And coming back to Poland, they took us to a room.
And they go-- went through everything.
They ripped off the soles from the shoes.
All the clothing was ripped, pieces, to-- looking for any documents.
But they took us as spies.
It was under the communists already.
When I walked into the room where they make the searches for us, I hanged up-- I had a little hat.
I hanged it up on the hook by the door.
And on the way out, I took the hat.
The guy saw it.
The military men what makes the search on me called me back.
He said, I did not look through your hat.
Shall I rip it apart?
I said, you did the whole clothing.
Why shall I care about the hat?
I throw it to him.
He throw it back.
In this hat, between the [? cards ?] inside the-- what you taking off it-- I had $50 hiding-- hidden.
And if they would caught the $50, I would get three years for this alone.
So I got back the-- [LAUGHS] --the hat.
But I tell them, I didn't care what you did to the whole clothing and the shoes.
Have this one too.
So he felt embarrassed.
He throwed it back to me.
So they took us, like that, to jail.
And they worked on us.
All of them, the friends what I went with them, told their story.
They give the pictures, by who they left the money.
They arrested all those people.
I said that there were two leaders.
I paid the other one.
And this guy didn't know.
I had no picture.
Had no money to give.
And they didn't find nothing by me.
They did not buy it.
They gave me beatings left and right.
The Polish.
And the communists, already.
The police, the investigators.
They beat me in the foot here and the sides.
And one day they called me in.
The investigator said, listen, you are the youngest between all of them.
Why you so stubborn?
Tell by who you left the money.
And we'll let you go free.
And I know that you're hungry.
You didn't eat already a day.
He gave me herring to eat.
And I was silly enough to eat.
I was very hungry.
With a small piece of bread, very salty herring.
And he said, you can go back to your room already.
And I went to the room back.
There was no water, anymore, there.
And 24 hours, nobody showed up to me.
I thought, I'll burn.
And after this, they called me in again for investigation.
The guy sits and sips water and ask questions.
I did not reveal for one reason.
The family was a friend with three little kids.
And I said, I will not put a man in jail if I know that he'll die.
But what-- what you do to get out of this?
After I went back to the cell-- cell-- and I was sitting in [? solitary ?] confinement, alone.
This already, four months, took at the time.
But the end of the four months-- after the herring episode, for this, a couple of days.
I said, I must find a way to get out of this.
I did it in time from the war, somehow.
I should find a way here.
The only thing with what to write, I had a comb for the hair.
I ripped-- pulled out a tooth.
And I wrote down a testament on the wall.
[LAUGHS] And I figured out how to do it.
Slowly, from the towers, I was pulling out threads.
And I made a rope.
The heating pipe was going with the ceiling.
At 7:00 in the morning, the guard was changing.
When the guard changed, one took over from the other.
They're both inspecting each cell.
From the solitary confinement, particularly.
I knew exactly based on the-- where they're working, what time it is, when, and what.
I-- after I wrote down the testament, that I cannot take it no longer, for after being so long in [? solitary ?] confinement, that I decided to end my life by hanging.
I put on the rope with the-- everything ready.
I jumped on the little table.
And a minute before they changed thing, I put it down.
And they both stand.
They caught me by the legs.
[LAUGHS]
So that time that took me out and put me back into the room which was about 10 or 11 people already.
I did not last five minute.
They took everybody out and left me alone.
But the guard didn't walk away from the door.
When I came back and all these people know me, they said, why you don't tell by who?
Why you decided to hang yourself?
You know?
It was--
I said, I cannot take it any longer.
And I don't need a life like this.
I have enough.
They took out each one in a separate room and took them for investigation.
What did I said when I come back?
So they said exactly what I told them.
They said no, the other guy said that I said that I did it specially.
They knew it's not true.
But-- So they all verified what I said.
And then they brought them back to the same room to me.
And each one tells their story.
And I figure myself, one slip of the tongue, if I would tell them what did it, would be the end of it.
And that time, they let me alone.
And I started, already, once a day, to walk out on the yard. 15 minute walking in circle, with all the prisoners.
That was, already, a big thing, to see a little bit fresh air from-- Going back to the [? solitary ?] confinement, when-- not to go out of my mind, I ripped off every button, what I have.
And I was laying on the floor and shooting.
Even now, I have a tendency to make something with papers.
And I-- actually, I can shoot straight to somebody-- [LAUGHS] -- on their finger.
This was laying on the floor.
I was making a mathematic calculations in the millions, just-- just to keep my mind rolling all the time.
So one day when I got back, now, to the work, when we're working in the back yard, I'm looking on these higher floor.
I see a tailor has the measurement, what they call it.
This was a soft measurement, what you wear it on this, the tailors.
He shows me, by the window, something like this to write.
I recognize that he tells-- he shows to me to write.
And I figure, and I say, what this man want from me?
I don't know him.
I come back and I'm thinking, maybe he wants me I shall write an application that I am a tailor.
And I did it.
I knock on the door.
The guard came over.
He said, what you want?
I said, I want to submit an application to give me paper, that I'm a tailor, to go.
He brought me paper.
I wrote it down.
And after half an hour, they took me out and brought me to this tailor for exam, to see if I'm a-- they needed tailors, all of them.
This was a shortage over there.
And they left me.
They said to the tailor, exam if he is-- if he knows how to sew.
And they walked out.
He said, you already passed the exam.
And his name was Mr.
Pollck.
I remember like this-- P-O-L-L-C-K.
I said, what make-- make you to show me to write?
He said, the fact that you understood what I meant is already good.
I got three years from a guy similar, like your story.
What he-- told him that he left him money by me.
One of your friends.
He told me who.
And the fact that you don't tell.
I said, I don't have what to tell.
They still didn't believe him.
I just said, I didn't-- he said, I don't want to know anything.
I want to make it easier, your life.
You will work with me now.
And I was working with him for two months, night time only going back to the cell.
One day they came, opened up the door.
They said, you already one week longer in jail what you should be.
I had a court, not knowing when and what.
All the time, when I was in jail, nobody knew where I am.
No letter.
Mother didn't know.
My girlfriend didn't know.
Disappeared for six months.
[LAUGHS]
I went out of jail.
This was around-- I know the war in Israel was already over. 1948, right after this.
I did not know the result that 90% of mine friends from Italy, they all fell on the way to Latrun.
On Latrun-- on the way to Jerusalem, if anybody saw the film [? that the youngsters from Cyprus came down over there.
And this was a true story. 90% of the guys what I was training, all died over there.
So this must be-- I went out of jail, probably in June, 1948.
So your being in jail might have saved your life?
Uh, probably, yes.
I didn't-- I didn't want her to stay in Poland.
Yes.
She remarried.
And her husband was buying eggs, chicken at that time. what-- peddler, like, buying and selling.
And my girlfriend was living next door to them at the time, also with her mother.
No I didn't-- not-- I know her.
But she wasn't my girlfriend yet, till I come back for my mother.
And I was several months in town.
And that's how we got to know each other.
I went to Warsaw.
I didn't want to live in a small town.
And I went to work in a-- actually, like, a corporate-- corporate factory, to make pocketbooks, women's purses.
I started with not knowing.
And each, by the table, was six people.
And each one was working according to percentage.
After two months I reached the equal percentage with the guys with their new [? girlfriend. ?] And I was there till July, 1950, in Warsaw, where I went to Israel.
Yes.
I went to visit many times.
And in July, 1950, we left legally from Poland with my mother.
Yes.
And I actually-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --put an application for her and her mother.
And she left one week later from me.
Szyfra.
Chamita-- C-H-A-M-I-T-A.
The picture tells everything.
We married in Israel.
We went-- farm-- we took a farm, Kfar Bilu, near Rehovot.
In July.
And our wedding was in December.
We went into a-- they call it like, a place where all the newcomers got together.
It was [HEBREW].
Like a passage-- passage place.
And I remember the first time they give us, to eat, olives.
And we start spitting out.
And we're from Poland, we're not used to olives to eat.
[LAUGHS] And we were looking for places.
And mine ex-friends from the Haganah, they recommended a town in Kfar Bilu, which is 20 kilometers from Tel Aviv.
That they are already 50 families.
There may be room for one more.
And that they're building the houses them self.
So I join them.
With mine girlfriend, already.
And we came over there, and we start building the home till that in December, we had the wedding.
We had no music, no light.
The soldiers were playing in cans from [?
Bisquick, ?] it was making music.
We pulled out [? vials ?] with kerosene lamp, for electricity.
And the next day, we went to [? cutting ?] [? oranges. ?] No honeymoon after the wedding.
And we had three kids in Israel, three daughters.
I was one of the successful farmers over there.
Actually, very successful.
Even today, when I come for a visit, they want to give me land to build a villa, a home.
No, own farm.
Private farm.
My brother, the Partisans did not release him.
They took him away to Russia.
I did not know, 30 years, if he is alive.
They-- he was a captain of the Russian army.
And they kept him in Kurilskiye Ostrava.
This is the island, what Russia took away from Japan, what Japan wants it back from them, even now.
And from there, this was military secret place.
And nobody could write a letter.
After 30 years, he wrote a letter to the Polish people we were hiding.
And that's-- I got news from them that he is alive.
Yes.
That's right.
And he wrote to them, and they found you.
Meanwhile, he married.
He already had a family in Russia.
We were-- did not know them.
At that time, he was the first immigrant from Russia to come to the States when I was here, thanks to you Senator Javits-- ex-- shall rest in peace.
I had an appointment.
Actually, his firm was mine attorney's in New York.
So I spoke to one of his co-workers.
I think attorney [?
Fersky ?] was his name.
He said, he'll have a meeting with the Russian ambassador, Dobrynin.
I shall give him all the details, the name, the addresses.
Took three weeks.
My brother, with the family, arrived.
This must have been in 1970, '72.
Well, there's-- Ted Koppel interviewed us on the airport when he arrived.
Ted Koppel from CBS, ABC.
More to that story.
And this was-- actually, he was the first immigrant.
He's now lives in Florida.
OK.
